petitioner_background: The petitioner is a sculptor and architect, apparently of Chinese origin based on the references to Chinese organizations, exhibitions, and documents. He sought classification as an individual of extraordinary ability in the arts under INA section 203(b)(1)(A). He worked as a design director and construction director for a company on more than 20 architectural projects involving residential and commercial buildings, and also created sculptures that were displayed in various exhibitions in China since the 1980s.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner submitted a portfolio containing photographs of various award certificates. This included a December 2010 certificate of honor from the editorial committee inviting him as a 'special master' for an arts exhibition commemorating an anniversary, and a December 2010 award certificate. He also submitted 13 additional certificates, including one indicating his work was 'selected to attend' an event (1990), one showing he 'supported and sponsored' through 'donation of' artwork, and a 2008 'Certificate of Participation' noting his work was displayed in an exhibition. In response to the RFE, he provided a single 'Certificate of Translation' from the translator listing seven items including 'Awards and Exhibitions.'
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO found that English language translations accompanying the portfolio were not certified by the translator as required by 8 C.F.R. § 103.2(b)(3). The single 'Certificate of Translation' submitted in response to the RFE did not specifically identify the documents it purportedly accompanied, and thus did not meet the regulatory requirement. Regardless of the translation deficiencies, the petitioner did not offer supporting evidence showing that any of the awards constitute nationally or internationally recognized awards for excellence in the field. The certificates appeared to be acknowledgments of participation in exhibitions or donation of artwork rather than recognized prizes for excellence in sculpting. The petitioner provided a listing and description of his 13 award certificates but did not submit accompanying evidence demonstrating national or international recognition.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner submitted membership credentials for two associations without certified English translations initially. In response to the RFE, he provided a document listing the application requirements: (1) agree with the constitution and will to join, (2) skilled craftsman with medium-grade professional title or above, (3) high school degree or above with at least five years work experience, (4) people with invention, significant science and technical achievement or monograph, (5) government leaders who support the career and work in arts and crafts area. On appeal, he submitted background information from the associations' respective websites.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO found that the single 'Certificate of Translation' did not specifically identify the documents and did not meet the regulatory requirement at 8 C.F.R. § 103.2(b)(3). Regardless, the five listed requirements did not rise to the level of 'outstanding achievements.' While the requirements included an 'invention, significant science and technical achievement or monograph,' the petitioner did not show that such work equated to 'outstanding achievements' in his field. The record did not include evidence demonstrating that members' achievements are judged by recognized national or international experts. The website background information submitted on appeal did not mention membership requirements.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner submitted a 2014 article in a publication, with an English language translation.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The English language translation was not certified by the translator as required by 8 C.F.R. § 103.2(b)(3), so the AAO could not determine whether the material was about the petitioner and relating to his work. Additionally, the author of the article was not identified, and the record did not include objective evidence showing that the publication is a professional or major trade publication or other form of major media.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner offered several letters of support. A letter from two professors at a Chinese arts academy stated that his work was collected by an institution and had been selected and awarded by various art exhibitions. A letter from a painter and sculptor stated the petitioner 'is a sculptor artist with [a] solid design foundation, intelligence and creativity' and that 'his success influences many young entrepreneurs and other artists,' and listed various works sculpted by the petitioner. An associate professor at a Chinese institution stated the petitioner 'is an artist with [a] gift' who since the 1980s attended various sculpture exhibitions and competitions. A chairman of the board and a senior engineer discussed the petitioner's work as a designer for their company, listing building decoration projects he oversaw. A senior craft artist and member of various Chinese arts organizations described projects including designing and making a logo and marine organism relief of granite, and decorating a building for an academician.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO noted that artistic exhibitions fall under a separate criterion (8 C.F.R. § 204.5(h)(3)(vii)), and meeting the display criterion is not presumptive evidence of original contributions of major significance. The reference letters did not explain how the petitioner's work has substantially impacted the visual arts field, influenced other artists' work, or equated to original contributions of major significance. Being a talented sculptor with others attesting to talent is not enough—an individual must have demonstrably impacted his field. The references listed works but did not provide specific examples of how the work affected others in the field or constituted original contributions of major significance. For work done for his employer, the plain language requires contributions of major significance 'in the field' rather than just to his employer and its projects. Generalized conclusory statements that do not identify specific contributions or their impact in the field have little probative value.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner provided an article in a publication. On appeal, he submitted a webpage providing information about a 'building decoration industry association.'
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The article was not accompanied by a full English language translation as required by 8 C.F.R. § 103.2(b)(3), so the AAO could not determine whether the article supported the petitioner's claims. The webpage submitted on appeal did not mention the publication in question. The petitioner did not provide supporting evidence demonstrating that the magazine is a professional or major trade publication or form of major media.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The record included photographs and certificates indicating that the petitioner's sculptures have been displayed at artistic exhibitions.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: Although the petitioner did not claim eligibility for this criterion on appeal, the AAO found that the record demonstrated his sculptures had been displayed at artistic exhibitions, and therefore he met this regulatory criterion.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner stated that as a design director for his company, he worked on many architectural projects designing both residential and commercial buildings, including large-scale projects creating cohesive designs for entire communities and compounds. He submitted a 'Projects table' prepared by the company reflecting his participation as 'design director' and 'construction director' on more than 20 projects. A letter from the company's chairman and a senior engineer stated the company had 'healthy and fast development under [the petitioner's] leading which brings abundant remuneration for our company and our coworkers.' He also provided four bid-winning notices reflecting that the company was selected to perform design projects.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO explained that a leading role is demonstrated by evidence of where a petitioner fits within the hierarchy and duties of an organization, while a critical role is demonstrated by contributions to operational viability. The petitioner did not provide an organizational chart or similar evidence to establish where his role fit within the overall hierarchy of the company. While he served as 'design director' and 'construction director' on various projects, he did not provide sufficient documentary evidence to support that his project duties were leading or critical for the company as a whole. The documentation did not differentiate him from other project directors and company executives, and did not establish that his work contributed in a way of substantial importance to the company's success. The letter from the chairman and senior engineer did not explain how his supervisory duties for various design projects translated to a leading role for the company as a whole or provide further information regarding financial success attributable to his work. Regarding the employer's distinguished reputation, the bid-winning notices showed the company was chosen as contractor but were not sufficient to show the company earned a distinguished reputation in the industry.

outcome_reasoning: The AAO dismissed the appeal, agreeing with the Director that the petitioner had not submitted the required initial evidence of either a one-time achievement or documents meeting at least three of the ten regulatory criteria. The petitioner only satisfied one criterion (exhibition). Because the petitioner failed to meet the antecedent regulatory requirement of presenting initial evidence under at least three criteria, a final merits determination under Kazarian was not required. Nevertheless, the AAO noted that a review of the record in the aggregate did not support a finding that the petitioner achieved the level of expertise required for this classification. The petitioner had not demonstrated by a preponderance of the evidence that he is an individual of extraordinary ability.
legal_citations: Rijal v. USCIS, 772 F. Supp. 2d 1339 (W.D. Wash. 2011), aff'd, 683 F.3d 1030 (9th Cir. 2012)
1756, Inc. v. U.S. Att'y Gen., 745 F. Supp. 9 (D.D.C. 1990)
fraud_or_procedural_issues: The petitioner repeatedly failed to comply with 8 C.F.R. § 103.2(b)(3), which requires certified English language translations of foreign language documents. His initial submissions lacked certified translations, and his response to the RFE included only a single 'Certificate of Translation' that listed seven general categories but did not specifically identify which documents it purportedly accompanied, failing to meet regulatory requirements. This translation deficiency affected multiple criteria including awards, membership, published material, and scholarly articles.
